coredata.dc:title: Size and refinement edge-shape effects of graphene quantum dots on UV–visible absorption
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Ruiqiang
Qi, Shifei
Jia, Jianfeng
Torre, Bryna
Zeng, Hao
Wu, Haishun
Xu, Xiaohong
coredata.dc:description: Using the ab initio density-functional theory method, we calculated the size effect and edge shape effect on UV–visible light absorption of different shapes of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). There are two interesting findings in this study. First, the edge shape effect increase with increasing the size of square GQDs. Second, the Seam atoms, located at the boundary between zigzag and armchair edges, hardly contribute to the strongest visible light absorption. This refinement of the edge-shape effect can be found in rectangular, triangular and hexagonal GQDs. This new finding will be useful in applications of GQDs in the visible light absorption nanodevices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanostructured materials
Computer simulations
Optical properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Size effect and edge shape effect

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimized ground-state geometry structures of (a) 4×3, (b) 3×4, (c) triangular, and (d) hexagonal GQDs. The 3×4 denotes the GQDs with 3 zigzag atoms and 4 armchair atoms. The Seam atom is indicated by the arrow. For 4×3, 3×4 and triangular GQDs we give part of the number of the side atoms. The small spheres in the figure are hydrogen atoms and big spheres are carbon atoms.

Absorption spectra of square GQDs of sized of 3×3, 4×4, 5×5, 6×6 and 7×7, respectively. Inset: the fitting results of the strongest absorption peak shift as a function of atomic number; the dot represents the calculated data and the star is the fitting result.

The charge distribution of HOMO for different sizes of GQDs (a) 3×3 GQDs, (b) 4×4 GQDs, (c) 5×5 GQDs, (d) 6×6 GQDs, (e) 7×7 GQDs, and (f) the percentages of the wave function of ZZ edge atoms in the HOMO and fitting lines for different sizes of square GQDs.
Absorption spectra (dash line) and the composite transition (vertical bars) of (a) the 4×3 rectangular GQDs and (b) the 3×4 rectangular GQDs in the visible light range.

The transition and related energy levels contributed by different edge atoms for (a) 4×3 GQDs, (b) 3×4 GQDs and (c) triangular GQDs, the density of states of all kinds of edge atoms are plotted in (d) 4×3 GQDs, (e) 3×4 GQDs and (f) triangular GQDs, H represents HOMO and L represents LUMO. The oscillator values of each transition are listed. The horizontal bars labeled as ZZ, AC and Seam are the averaged contributions of zigzag, armchair and Seam atoms respectively. In (c), the enlarged sketch is used to show the details of transitions of 1, 2, 5 and 6. In (d–f) the bold solid line represents the density of states of Seam atoms, and the vertical dash line represents the HOMO level.
The charge distribution of HOMO and LUMO for different shaped GQDs (a) 4×3 GQDs HOMO, (b) 4×3 GQDs LUMO, (c) 3×4 GQDs HOMO, (d) 3×4 GQDs LUMO, (e) triangular GQDs HOMO, and (f) triangular GQDs LUMO.
The nearest neighbor C–C bond length of edge atoms indicated in Fig. 1, for 4×3 GQD, 3×4 GQD and triangular GQD.

head.ce:title: Size and refinement edge-shape effects of graphene quantum dots on UV–visible absorption

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen 041004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Magnetic Molecules and Magnetic Information Materials, Ministry of Education, Linfen 041004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Magnetic Molecules and Magnetic Information Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, University at Buffalo, The State University of New York, Buffalo, NY 14260, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The State University of New York

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen 041004, PR China. Tel.: +86 357 2051706; fax: +86 357 2052468.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using the ab initio density-functional theory method, we calculated the size effect and edge shape effect on UV–visible light absorption of different shapes of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). There are two interesting findings in this study. First, the edge shape effect increase with increasing the size of square GQDs. Second, the Seam atoms, located at the boundary between zigzag and armchair edges, hardly contribute to the strongest visible light absorption. This refinement of the edge-shape effect can be found in rectangular, triangular and hexagonal GQDs. This new finding will be useful in applications of GQDs in the visible light absorption nanodevices.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene nano materials show promising applications in many fields such as solar cells [1,2], nano-electrochemical systems [3], nanoelectronics [4], and mechanical alloying [5,6]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is an important graphene nanomaterial that is predicted to useful in photodynamic therapy [7] solar cells [8] and ion detection devices [9]. In these nanoscale devices, the physical properties of GQDs may be tuned by the size effect [10–15] and the shape effect [16–21]. For example, the zigzag (ZZ) and armchair (AC) edges of graphene show different electronic and optical properties [21,22].

Numerous works were conducted on the size and edge-shape effects in graphene and other carbon-based materials, such as nanotubes [23,24], nanoribbons [25,26] and quantum dots [22,27–29]. Most of these works only considered the shape effect of the nanoflakes or the states of the zigzag edges of GQDs. Further, they only reported the influence of shape effect on mechanical properties, fluorescence quenching, and electronic structures [5,12,21]. To our knowledge, the special atoms which are at the boundary between zigzag and armchair edges have not been studied.

In this paper, we investigate the visible light absorption of different sizes and shapes of GQDs and quantitatively analyze the contribution of each kind of edge atoms to the energy grade and the density of state (DOS). Especially, we find that the atoms located at the boundary between zigzag and armchair edges contribute the least to the visible light absorption among all edge atoms. Hence, we suggest that the number of these atoms should be minimized if the GQDs are to be application in solar absorption.

We constructed a series of square GQDs, denoted as N × N (N ZZ × N AC, N =3, 4, 5, 6, 7), to examine the size effects. N represents the number of atoms on the zigzag or armchair side. As shown in Fig. 1 , we also constructed rectangular, triangular, and hexagonal GQDs to study the shape effects. All density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed using the Gaussian 09 software package [30]. The absorption properties were calculated by time-dependent DFT (TDDFT) calculations [31–33] with 6-31G (d) basis set. We used the hybrid exchange–correlation functions (B3LYP) [34], as a result of its good representation of the characteristic of electronic structure of C-based systems [35,36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Size effect and edge shape effect
ce:section.ce:para:
In this study, we systematically calculated the UV–visible absorption spectra of square GQDs of various sizes, including 3×3, 4×4, 5×5, 6×6, and 7×7. Each square GQD is composed of the same number of ZZ and AC edge atoms. The dangling bonds are passivated by the hydrogen atoms. Fig. 2 shows the absorption spectra of the square GQDs with different sizes. The absorption wavelengths in the UV–visible region change with the GQD size. The main peak shifts to the longer wavelength region with the increasing GQD size, indicating that the size affects the absorption of square GQDs. The strongest absorption peaks of the 3×3, 4×4, 5×5, 6×6, 7×7 GQDs are located at 206.7, 284.1, 335.7, 359.7, and 393.9nm, respectively, as shown in Fig. 2. The shift of the strongest absorption peak with the total atomic number Na can be fitted by using
36.97 ×
N
a
, which is consist with the result [28,29]
E
gap
∝ 1 /
N
a
, where E gap is the energy gap of the GQD. The atomic number Na includes both carbon and hydrogen atoms in the GQD. The solid circles in Fig. 2 (inset) represent the positions of the strongest peaks of the calculated absorption spectra in Fig. 2, and the star represents the fitting results. It can be seen that they match quite well. From Fig. 2 (inset), we deduce that the strongest absorption peak shifts from 207nm to 730nm when the atomic number of square GQDs increases from 30atoms to 390atoms. The relationship of the total atom number Na and the edge size N satisfies the equation Na
=2 N (N +2). The total atom number of 390 corresponds to a 13×13 GQD. Hence, the GQDs can be expected to encompass a full solar spectrum absorption by controlling the size of the square GQDs in solar cells.

The edge shape plays an important role because the fraction of edge atoms is greatly enhanced in small graphene flakes. Theoretical calculations determined that a localized edge state appears at the Fermi level in graphene ZZ ribbons. This edge state has a key effect on electronic and magnetic properties [28,37,38]. Considering the existence of both ZZ and AC edges in the rectangular GQDs, we focus on how these two kinds of edges affect the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). Fig. 3 (a–e) shows the HOMO of different kinds of GQDs from 3×3 to 7×7 respectively. The atomic percentage of ZZ edges in the HOMO for square GQDs with different sizes were shown in Fig. 3f. With the increase in GQD size from 3×3 to 7×7, the percentages of the wave function of ZZ edge atoms in the HOMO increase from 69.76% to 91.05%. We can see that the percentage of ZZ edge state in HOMO increased monotonically with N increasing. The change in percentage can be fitted very well by using 77.50×(1− e −
N
/3)+21, which means that the contribution to the HOMO come from atoms other than those on the zigzag edges that will decrease exponentially with the increasing of GQD size. However, the contribution from no zigzag edge atoms will never decrease to zero, therefore the percentage come from zigzag edges will approach but never reach 100%. This variation has two implications: (i) HOMO is mainly affected by the ZZ sides belonging to the edge-shape effect, and more importantly, (ii) the edge-shape effect is accompanied by size effects and varies with dimension.

Then, we present how the ZZ and AC edges affect the GQD optoelectronic properties in the visible range. We constructed two types of rectangular GQDs with the same number of carbon and hydrogen atoms to exclude the size effect. The two types are the 4×3 (Fig. 1a) and 3×4 (Fig. 1b) GQDs. The absorbance and corresponding transitions for the 4×3 and 3×4 GQDs are denoted by the dashed line and bars in Fig. 4 . Both GQDs have 24 carbon atoms and 14 hydrogen atoms each, but the 4×3 GQD has five composite transitions in the visible light range, whereas 3×4 GQD only has three. The 4×3 has more absorption peaks in the visible light range than the 3×4. Hence, we can say that the ZZ localized edge state of GQDs is very important for the light absorption in the visible light range.

The functions of the ZZ and AC GQD edges have been studied extensively. Generally, it is considered that there are two types of edge atoms ZZ and AC in GQDs. In this paper, we focus on the atoms occupying the boundary sites between ZZ and AC sides. We call these atoms Seam atoms (indicated by the arrow in Fig. 1). Further analysis of the individual edge atoms indicates that the Seam atoms and other edge atoms have very different effects on visible light absorption. We analyzed the strongest transition of each absorption peak of 4×3 and 3×4 GQDs, as shown Fig. 5 (a and b). The corresponding oscillator strengths and the contribution of the electronic wave function of distinct edge atoms to the transition-related energy levels are shown using different colored 1 For interpretation of color in Fig. 5, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.
1 bars. The edge atoms are classified as ZZ edge atoms, AC edge atoms and Seam atoms. The percentage values of the bars are averaged over all the edge atoms. Only the oscillator strength greater than 0.01 are considered. In Fig. 5a, there are five strong transitions that relate to six energy levels: H-5, H-1, H, L, L+1 and L+2. Among the six energy levels H-1 and L+2 were determined to be associated with the Seam atoms within the visible light transition. The contribution of the wave function of the Seam atoms for the H-1 energy level is less than that of the other edge atoms based on a comparison of the lengths of the three horizontal bars. Although the contribution of the wave function of the Seam atoms to the L+2 energy level is the largest among those of the three energy levels, the L+2 energy level is only relevant to the second transition, and the corresponding calculated oscillator strength value of 0.02 is much smaller than the other four oscillator values of 0.19, 0.51, 0.59, and 0.07. The H-2 energy level does not take part in any of the five transitions, and yet the energy level strongly related with Seam atoms. Thus the three energy levels relating to the Seam atoms, H-2, H-1, and L+2 contribute little to the visible light absorption. However, from the averaged contribution value of ZZ edge atoms and AC edge atoms we can see that the ZZ edge atom contributes to the H-5, H-1, H, L, L+1 energy levels significantly, and the energy levels H-1, H, L, L+1, L+2 related with the AC edge atoms strongly. Therefore, the Seam atoms hardly participate in the strongest transition in the visible light absorption process. We call this phenomenon the refinement edge-shape effect.

For the 3×4 GQDs, three transitions exist in the visible range, as shown in Fig. 5b. Considering that the oscillator strength of the third transition is one order of magnitude larger than that of the other two, we only discuss the third transition. The third transition occurs in the H-1 and L+1 energy level, but the Seam atoms do not affect either of them. Conversely, the averaged contribution value of ZZ edge atoms and AC edge atoms clearly shows that both ZZ and AC edge atom contribute to the H-1 and L+1 energy level. Hence the Seam atoms have little effect on the light absorption in the 3×4 GQDs relative to the other edge atoms.

We also calculated the triangular GQD (Fig. 1c), which has the same number of carbon and hydrogen atoms as the rectangle GQDs. All three sides of the triangular GQD are ZZ edges. As shown in Fig. 5c, seven transitions exist, which is in agreement with the preceding conclusion that the localized state of ZZ sides provides greater chances for the electron transition. All seven transitions correspond to eight energy levels, including H-5, H-3, H-1, H, L, L+1, L+3, and L+5. Based on the contribution of the different edge atoms to each transition energy level, the eight energy levels are not affected by the Seam atom, which means that the Seam atom have no participation in the visible light absorption process, while other edge atoms take part in the visible light absorption to varying extents .

For the hexagonal GQD (Fig. 1d), there are no ZZ edge states, as they require at least three continued ZZ edge atoms. For this GQD, no transition occurs in the visible range because of the edge-shape effect. Hence, this result is in agreement with those for the triangular and rectangular GQDs. The preceding discussion confirms that the refinement edge-shape effect clearly exists in the visible light absorption of GQDs.

Fig. 5(d–f) shows the density of states for all types of edge atoms, where the other edge atom correspond to such atoms one by one. A dashed-line represents the HOMO level in the middle of the three figures, and the bold solid lines emphasize the density of state of Seam atoms. We can see that the Seam atoms contribute little to the energy levels near the HOMO and LUMO. The Seam atoms contribute to the energy levels far away from the HOMO and LUMO, but energy of transition related to these energy levels exceeds the visible light range. On the other hand, there is a clear contribution from the other edge atoms on the energy levels near the HOMO and LUMO.

The refinement edge-shape effect can be understood by comparing the relative stability of the Seam atoms with those of the other edge atoms. The relative stability of different atoms in a molecule is reflected by the bond lengths. As seen in Table 1 , the bond length between the Seam atom and neighbouring C atom is shorter than that of the other edge atoms, which indicates that the Seam atoms are more stable than the other edge atoms. Therefore, the other edge atoms most likely participate in light absorption when the GQDs are excited by the visible light.

Next, in Fig. 6 we show the charge distribution of the HOMO and LUMO for different shaped GQDs in order to further explain the differences from the edge atoms. The Seam atoms make almost no contribution to HOMO and LUMO, while the average contribution from other zigzag edge atoms to the HOMO and LUMO is larger than all the other carbon atoms. Based on the Frontier Orbital Theory [39], the small contribution of the Seam atoms to HOMO and LUMO explains why these atoms make a small contribution to the visible absorption spectrum. Alternatively, there are some carbon atoms with one C–C bond nearest to the end of the ZZ edge in our GQDs (labeled 1 in Figs. 1a and 5 in Fig. 1b and c). They contribute more to the HOMO and LUMO than the other edge atoms. This is because their single C–C bond makes them more active than the other edge atoms. These zigzag edge atoms will greatly affect the properties that are related to the HOMO and LUMO, such as catalysis and optoelectronic process [40,41].

Realizing how such small GQDs and forming exact ZZ and AC edge is still a great challenge. Kosimov et al. [42] calculated the stable structure of Cn (n ⩽50). They found that the H passivation can change the ground state configurations. After the H passivated the edge carbon atom, the graphene flake was more stable. In our paper, most of the nanodots are metastable configurations, however, it is possible to realize these nanostructures. Müllen group [43] introduces the versatile syntheses of 2D graphene molecules and potential application for electronics. The bottom-up [44] method can precisely fabricate the armchair edge of graphene nanoribbons. There are still other methods that could be used to fabricate GQDs such as Joule heating of nanoribbons [45], electron bombardment [46], or specific etching techniques [47].

Therefore, based on TDDFT calculations, the size effects and the edge-shape effect of GQDs are confirmed to be significant for the visible light absorption properties of GQDs. The edge-shape effect is intertwined with the size effect, and is enhanced with an increase in size of square GQDs. The Seam atoms are inactive in the strongest transition peak within the visible light range. In designing optoelectronic devices using GQDs, this refinement edge-shape effect, should be considered because the Seam atoms occupy a large proportion of arbitrarily shaped GQDs. The effects of Seam atoms should be minimized to enhance the light absorption in GQDs by accurately controlling the shape.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Our research was supported by the NSFC (No. 51025101, 11274214 and 11104173), the Ministry of Education of China (IRT 1156 and RFDPHE 20121404130001), the special funds of Shanxi Scholars program and US NSF DMR-1104994. We would also like to thank Peihong Zhang for critical reading of our paper.

Fox, Gaussian, Inc., Wallingford, CT, 2009.
